summary:
The Tenerians (Tenerian culture) were a Neolithic people of the southern Sahara, centered in the Air region of present-day Niger, dating from approximately 5000 to 2500 BCE. They inhabited the Sahara during the African Humid Period, when the region received significantly more rainfall than today and supported a diverse ecosystem of lakes, rivers, grasses, and woodland. The Tenerians left behind burials, rock art, and material assemblages that provide important evidence for human life in the ancient Green Sahara.

The most striking evidence for the Tenerians comes from the site of Gobero in northern Niger, excavated by Paul Sereno and colleagues beginning in 2000. Gobero preserves two overlapping cemeteries — one Kiffian (an earlier culture dating to c. 8000–6000 BCE) and one Tenerian — in the dried bed of a former lake. The Tenerian burials include elaborately arranged interments, including a famous triple burial of a woman with two children interred embracing each other, suggesting deep emotional bonds and careful mortuary practices.

The Tenerians were hunter-gatherers and early pastoralists, herding cattle and hunting the abundant wildlife of the humid Sahara including hippos, crocodiles, and large ungulates. As the Sahara began to dry after 3000 BCE, the Tenerians — like other Saharan cultures — were displaced southward or eastward, contributing to the complex pattern of population movements that shaped the cultural landscape of sub-Saharan Africa.
